title: Eigencurve over the boundary of the weight space
speaker: Liang Xiao
abstract: Eigencurve was introduced by Coleman and Mazur to parametrize modular forms varying $p$-adically. It is a rigid analytic curve such that each point corresponds to an overconvegent eigenform. In this talk, we discuss a conjecture on the geometry of the eigencurve: over the boundary annuli of the weight space, the eigencurve breaks up into infinite disjoint union of connected components and the weight map is finite and flat on each component. This was first verified by Buzzard and Kilford by an explicit computation in the case of $p = 2$ and tame level 1.

proof_stages.label: Weight characters and the boundary of weight space
proof_stages.summary: The talk combines classical weight and the p-part of the Nebentype character into a continuous character of Z_p^\times, identifies weight space with finitely many open disks via T=\kappa(1+p)-1, and interprets the boundary as the region of highly ramified p-power conductor characters.

proof_stages.label: Fredholm construction of the spectral curve
proof_stages.summary: Using Banach spaces of overconvergent modular forms S_\kappa with compact U_p action, the speaker forms a universal Fredholm series over weight space whose zero locus records possible U_p eigenvalues and defines the spectral curve/eigencurve.

proof_stages.label: Halo problem and model computation
proof_stages.summary: The Coleman–Mazur question about the eigencurve near the boundary, together with the Buzzard–Kilford computed case, motivates expecting a near-boundary decomposition into independent pieces with slopes controlled by the weight parameter.

proof_stages.label: Quaternionic halo decomposition
proof_stages.summary: For a definite quaternion algebra split at p and neat tame level, the main theorem decomposes the spectral curve over the annulus |T|>1/p into finite-flat pieces X_I indexed by integer points and intervening intervals, with v_p(U_p) constrained by (p-1)v_p(T)I and hence tending to zero at the boundary.

proof_stages.label: Degree formulas, periodicity, and classical consequences
proof_stages.summary: The theorem further computes the degrees of the pieces over each weight component in terms of weight-two and ordinary dimensions, shows a Teichmüller-twist periodicity in these degrees, and explains the resulting near-even distribution of finite classical slopes at high p-power conductor weights.

proof_stages.label: Straight-line refinement deeper in the boundary
proof_stages.summary: After restricting farther toward the boundary, to an explicit annulus |T|>\lambda, each X_I can be refined so that the slope ratio v_p(U_p)/((p-1)v_p(T)) is constant on each subpiece, and the resulting ratios form a finite union of arithmetic progressions.

proof_stages.label: Integral boundary model proof mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The proof avoids treating boundary affinoids separately by constructing an integral model of overconvergent forms over a boundary algebra such as Z_p⟨T,pT^{-1}⟩, where analysis of the U_p Fredholm series yields the decomposition and slope estimates.

examples.name: Algebraic weights in the center of weight space
examples.note: The computation T(x^k)=(1+p)^k-1 shows that weights with trivial p-part Nebentype lie in the small central disk, making the weight-space coordinate concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker computes the t-coordinate and its p-adic norm for algebraic characters x^k and uses this to locate them in the central radius of the weight disk.

passes.reason: They are classical central weights with trivial wild p-level/Nebentypus, used only as a contrast case.
The algebraic weights have trivial p-power Nebentype/minimal p-level and are used only to illustrate the central region of weight space.
Pure algebraic weights have trivial wild Nebentype/no p-level and are used only to mark the central locus.
These are the no-p-Nebentype cyclotomic weights, a minimal-conductor central case used only for contrast.
pure algebraic weights have trivial p-power Nebentype/no p-level and are used only as the central comparison case

Trivial-at-p algebraic weights have minimal p-level and are used only as the central contrast to the boundary.
Trivial-p-level algebraic weights form a minimal central family used only as contrast.
They are used only for contrast, and trivial p-Nebentype/no p-level makes them the minimally ramified central weights.

these locally algebraic weights have trivial p-power Nebentypus/no p-level, so they are minimal-conductor central weights used only for contrast

examples.name: Primitive p^m Nebentype characters near the boundary
examples.note: A p-primitive character psi_m sends 1+p to a p^{m-1}-st root of unity, so its T-coordinate is much farther from the center and tends toward the boundary as m grows.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker analyzes a p-primitive character psi_m by tracking the image of 1+p, computing the resulting t-coordinate and norm, and using this to place these characters near the boundary.

passes.reason: Primitive conductor p^m characters have maximal p-power conductor for their modulus and move toward the weight-space boundary.
Primitive conductor-p^m Nebentype characters are maximally ramified at that modulus and move toward the boundary of weight space, not used to refute a claim.
Primitive conductor p^m characters lie in the high-conductor locus tending to the boundary of weight space.
Primitive conductor-p^m Nebentype characters are maximally p-ramified for their modulus and tend toward the boundary.
p-primitive conductor-p^m characters are maximally ramified for their modulus and lie toward the boundary of weight space
Primitive characters of conductor p^m have maximal conductor modulo p^m and lie toward the boundary of weight space.
Primitive conductor-p^m Nebentypus characters give finite-order weights whose coordinates tend toward the boundary as m grows, illustrating the boundary regime.
Primitive conductor-p^m Nebentype characters are highly ramified weights tending to the boundary, used illustratively.
Primitive conductor-p^m Nebentype characters are high-ramification weights approaching the boundary, not an obstruction.
They illustrate boundary weights, with primitive high p-power conductor forcing them toward the edge of weight space.
primitive conductor-p^m characters lie on annuli tending to the boundary of weight space
primitive p^m characters have maximal conductor modulo p^m and, as m grows, lie at the boundary of weight space

examples.name: Buzzard–Kilford p=2 tame level one computation
examples.note: Their explicit computation, where the eigencurve near the boundary is a union of annuli with slope ratios 0,1,2,..., is presented as the model suggesting the general halo decomposition.
examples.engagement_reason: The Buzzard–Kilford p=2 tame level one case is cited as a known computation and its conclusions are summarized, but no computation or construction is carried out on it in the talk.

passes.reason: The Buzzard–Kilford case is the minimal p=2, tame-level-one/full-level base case cited as a model computation.
The Buzzard–Kilford case is the minimal tame-level-one p=2 base case, cited as a motivating computation rather than an obstruction.
The p=2, tame-level-one eigencurve is a minimal-level edge case with a maximally regular degree-one annular halo.
The p=2 tame-level-one case is a minimal-level base case with an unusually regular degree-one annular halo.

The p=2 tame-level-one case is a minimal-level base case whose halo components map with minimal degree one, cited as a positive prototype.
The p=2 tame-level-one case is a minimal/no-tame-level, highly regular eigencurve computation used as a motivating special case, not as a failure example.
The Buzzard–Kilford case is the minimal p=2, tame-level-one/no-extra-level computation motivating the theorem.
The p=2 tame-level-one case is minimal-level and has a maximally regular degree-one annular halo used as a motivating computation.
It is cited as supporting prior computation in the minimal p=2, tame-level-one base case with unusually regular annular components.

examples.name: Classical high-conductor weight slice
examples.note: Taking a classical weight x^k psi_m and invoking Coleman classicality illustrates how the theorem predicts the vertical distribution of classical finite slopes at large p-power level.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker specializes to a classical weight x^k psi_m with high p-conductor, intersects it with the spectral curve, and applies the theorem/Coleman classicality to describe the resulting slope distribution.
